jokes.text: Enjoy your multicultural hooey.
jokes.setup: Willie is introducing what's supposed to be an educational diversity film
jokes.punchline: His blunt dismissal of it as 'hooey' while still required to show it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Willie characterization - forced to show educational content he clearly despises. The word 'hooey' is perfectly chosen for his curmudgeonly personality.

jokes.text: Your most beautiful costume is your self-esteem. // Pause here to discuss tolerance. What is this crap?
jokes.setup: Saccharine educational film dialogue about self-esteem
jokes.punchline: Immediate student reaction calling it crap right after the pause prompt
jokes.explanation: Good timing joke - the earnest educational content immediately undercut by honest student reaction

jokes.text: This is worse than 'wheelchair-nocchio.'
jokes.setup: Student complaining about the bad educational film
jokes.punchline: Reference to a fictional politically correct version of Pinocchio
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that perfectly captures overly-PC educational material. 'Wheelchair-nocchio' is brilliantly constructed.

jokes.text: Hey, cryptkeeper, I like your Dodge scare-a-van.
jokes.setup: Bart sees Mr. Burns' old car
jokes.punchline: Combines 'Cryptkeeper' insult with 'scare-a-van' pun on Caravan
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart insult with good wordplay. The double pun works well and fits his character perfectly.

jokes.text: It was the first car to outrun a man. A caveman.
jokes.setup: Burns defending his ancient car as historically significant
jokes.punchline: The clarification that it was a caveman makes it pathetically slow
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns moment - his extreme old age humor with excellent comedic timing on the clarification

jokes.text: I thought of that 'cause I slept in a cave last night.
jokes.setup: Following the caveman reference
jokes.punchline: Student's completely random personal revelation
jokes.explanation: Weak random humor - feels disconnected and doesn't add much comedic value

jokes.text: Kill them, Smithers. // You could just buy a new car, sir. // Well... whatever's easier.
jokes.setup: Burns wants revenge on the children for mocking him
jokes.punchline: His casual consideration that murder might be easier than car shopping
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns-Smithers dynamic. Burns' casual evil paired with Smithers' practical suggestion creates perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: May I suggest an Oldsmobile?
jokes.setup: Burns needs a new car recommendation
jokes.punchline: Smithers suggests the most stereotypically old person car
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment - Smithers knows his boss well enough to suggest the most age-appropriate vehicle

jokes.text: Now, hold perfectly still for 78 minutes.
jokes.setup: Burns wants to take a photo of the kids for revenge
jokes.punchline: His camera requires an impossibly long exposure time
jokes.explanation: Great Burns antiquity joke - the specific time '78 minutes' makes it funnier than just 'a long time'

jokes.text: That's the car I think about when I make love to my wife.
jokes.setup: Burns mentions getting a Lamborgotti Fasterossa
jokes.punchline: Smithers' disturbing overshare about his fantasy life
jokes.explanation: Unexpected TMI from Smithers that's both uncomfortable and funny. Good character moment showing his obsessions.

jokes.text: That guy in first class is taking a tv out of his armrest. // What's in you? // A bunch of stupid cables.
jokes.setup: Kids observing first class amenities while in coach
jokes.punchline: Lisa's armrest contains only cables, highlighting the class disparity
jokes.explanation: Decent airplane class commentary. The contrast works but isn't particularly original.

jokes.text: Cletus, you are the most wonderful husband and son I ever had.
jokes.setup: Brandine praising Cletus for getting the kids a playhouse
jokes.punchline: Implies Cletus is both her husband and son (incest)
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons dark humor about rural stereotypes. Perfectly economical delivery of a shocking implication.

jokes.text: Well, some people in Europe have the impression that America has made some stupid choices in the past, oh, five years.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks about the Canadian flag on Homer's backpack
jokes.punchline: Homer's understated reference to Bush-era foreign policy disasters
jokes.explanation: Good political satire with Homer's characteristic understatement. The 'oh, five years' timing is perfect.

jokes.text: That flag is mine! Don't mess with Texas! Shock and awe, losers! Shock and awe!
jokes.setup: Homer was trying to pass as Canadian to avoid anti-American sentiment
jokes.punchline: Immediately blows his cover with aggressive American patriotism
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - can't help himself from being exactly what he was trying not to be. Good escalation.

jokes.text: Why don't you encourage him to get us some health insurance?
jokes.setup: Marge encouraging Homer to be irresponsible for once
jokes.punchline: Lisa points out more pressing family needs
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa moment - practical and socially aware, cutting through the vacation fantasy with reality.

jokes.text: A McDonald's where you can get booze!
jokes.setup: Homer saying he can't experience Italy until he's there
jokes.punchline: What amazes him most about Italy is alcohol at McDonald's, not cultural sites
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - completely missing the cultural significance for something food/drink related.

jokes.text: But, dad, don't you want to turn around and see the tower? // There's a picture of it on my cup.
jokes.setup: Lisa wants Homer to see the Leaning Tower of Pisa
jokes.punchline: Homer thinks the McDonald's cup photo is sufficient
jokes.explanation: Good Homer anti-culture moment. His logic is perfectly stupid and in-character.

jokes.text: Savages.
jokes.setup: Learning about Pompeii victims frozen by volcanic ash
jokes.punchline: Homer calls the disaster victims 'savages' for no logical reason
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing and Homer ignorance. One word that completely misses the tragedy.

jokes.text: It says here we should turn left at a fat chick in a tutu being fed by a midget. // That's a dvd of a Fellini movie.
jokes.setup: Homer consulting the navigation system while lost
jokes.punchline: He's describing a Fellini film as driving directions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual comedy premise - Homer confusing art film imagery with GPS directions. Great cultural reference.

jokes.text: I get it. The midget represents dwarves.
jokes.setup: Just learned he was looking at a Fellini movie
jokes.punchline: Homer's completely missing-the-point analysis of film symbolism
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the Fellini joke - Homer trying to sound smart about art but completely missing the point.

jokes.text: Mozzarella! Parmigiano! Provolone! Pecorino! Gorgonzola! Fontina! Taleggio! Bocconcini!
jokes.setup: Homer crashes into a cheese truck
jokes.punchline: His excited listing of all the cheese types he's encountering
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer food obsession. The enthusiasm during a car accident is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: We got the cheese insurance. // It doesn't cover mortadella. // No!
jokes.setup: Homer worried about the cheese truck damage
jokes.punchline: The insurance has oddly specific exclusions for Italian meats
jokes.explanation: Absurd insurance joke with perfect escalation. The specific exclusion makes it funnier than general coverage.

jokes.text: Wish you'd never been unified by Victor Emmanuel II. If only you'd stayed a loose confederation of city-states, trading with each other and occasionally warring.
jokes.setup: Homer frustrated with Italy after the accident
jokes.punchline: His surprisingly detailed knowledge of Italian unification history in his rant
jokes.explanation: Brilliant unexpected erudition from Homer. The contrast between his usual ignorance and this specific historical knowledge is perfect.

jokes.text: Why can't you people learn to speak my language? I learned to eat your food.
jokes.setup: Homer frustrated that Italians don't speak English
jokes.punchline: His logic that eating Italian food should create reciprocal language obligation
jokes.explanation: Perfect ugly American tourist behavior from Homer. His quid pro quo logic is brilliantly stupid.

jokes.text: Kentucky?! In italia, this a-means 'whore'!
jokes.setup: Homer wearing Kentucky shirt introduces himself
jokes.punchline: Villager's shock at what the word means in Italian
jokes.explanation: Decent cultural misunderstanding joke, though the premise is a bit contrived.

jokes.text: Sideshow Bob of all the regioni and all the villaggi in all of Italia, you had to be il mayore of this one!
jokes.setup: Recognizing the Simpsons have arrived in his village
jokes.punchline: Parody of Casablanca's famous line with Italian words mixed in
jokes.explanation: Good movie reference parody. Bob's theatrical nature makes the Casablanca reference perfect for his character.

jokes.text: But it better have a beginning, a middle and an end. And you'd better make us root for the protagonist.
jokes.setup: Bob about to tell his story
jokes.punchline: Lisa giving him strict narrative requirements like a writing teacher
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-joke about storytelling conventions. Perfect for Lisa's intellectual character.

jokes.text: Okay, so far I'm rooting for you.
jokes.setup: Bob mentions his tale begins with trying to murder Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart's immediate approval of his own attempted murder
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark humor. Bart's casual attitude toward his own attempted murder is brilliantly twisted.

jokes.text: It's pronounced 'buon giorno.' Your mouth is getting lazy on the second syllable. Here, let me guide you.
jokes.setup: Bob learning Italian from locals
jokes.punchline: His perfectionist nature applied to pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bob's intellectual pretensions even while learning basic Italian.

jokes.text: These feet are made for stomping and that's just what they'll do one of these days these feet will make chianti out of you ready, feet? Start stomping.
jokes.setup: Bob grape stomping in the vineyard
jokes.punchline: Parody of Nancy Sinatra's 'These Boots Are Made for Walkin' adapted for winemaking
jokes.explanation: Clever song parody that fits Bob's theatrical personality. The adaptation works well for the grape stomping context.

jokes.text: Holy moley! I always thought you were, you know, out loud and proud. // Well, I experimented in college, as one does.
jokes.setup: Bob introduces his wife and child
jokes.punchline: Bob's casual dismissal of his apparent gay experimentation
jokes.explanation: Subverts character assumptions with Bob's casual attitude about past experimentation.

jokes.text: I never went to college. // Stop the presses.
jokes.setup: Homer's response to Bob's college reference
jokes.punchline: Bob's sarcastic fake surprise at Homer's lack of education
jokes.explanation: Good character interplay showing Bob's intellectual snobbery toward Homer.

jokes.text: Bart Simpson! Bart Simpson! Bart Simpson! I make like my daddy!
jokes.setup: Bob's young son meeting Bart for the first time
jokes.punchline: The child imitating his father's murderous tendencies toward Bart
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny hereditary evil moment. The innocent child's eagerness to murder is perfectly twisted.

jokes.text: Bart and I used to go fly-fishing together. // Die, Bart! Die, Bart!
jokes.setup: Bob reminiscing about bonding with Bart
jokes.punchline: His son's death chant contrasting with the peaceful activity
jokes.explanation: Great contrast between wholesome father-son activity and murderous intent. The juxtaposition works perfectly.

jokes.text: He makes love like a man who just got out of jail.
jokes.setup: Francesca talking about her wonderful Roberto
jokes.punchline: Her inadvertently accurate description of Bob's criminal background
jokes.explanation: Brilliant double meaning - romantic to her, literal truth about Bob's prison experience. Perfect ironic comedy.

jokes.text: Surely, even the most heinous criminal deserves a seventh chance.
jokes.setup: Bob pleading for the Simpsons not to expose him
jokes.punchline: The absurdly high number of chances he's already had
jokes.explanation: 'Seventh chance' perfectly captures Bob's recurring villain status while maintaining his eloquent speaking style.

jokes.text: Not necessarily in words, but in body language. You know, sneaking around and such.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming he tells Marge everything
jokes.punchline: His definition of communication is terrible sneaking behavior
jokes.explanation: Good Homer relationship humor showing his complete misunderstanding of honest communication.

jokes.text: You can't be a bad person if you have a family.
jokes.setup: Marge defending Bob's redemption
jokes.punchline: Her naive logic that family automatically equals goodness
jokes.explanation: Classic Marge naivety. The statement is both sweet and completely wrong, perfect for her character.

jokes.text: from Jean Valjean to the voice of Buzz Lightyear, Tim Allen.
jokes.setup: Lisa citing examples of redemption in literature
jokes.punchline: Going from classic French literature to a Disney voice actor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant highbrow/lowbrow juxtaposition. Lisa's intellectual pretensions undercut by pop culture reference.

jokes.text: Dad, don't act like Mussolini. // I thought I was doing Donald Trump.
jokes.setup: Homer gesturing dramatically to the Italian crowd
jokes.punchline: Homer's confusion about which authoritarian figure he's imitating
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire comparing Trump to fascist dictators. Homer's confusion makes it funnier.

jokes.text: This sausage shop has been grinding the same family of pigs for 600 years.
jokes.setup: Bob giving a cute fact about the local sausage shop
jokes.punchline: The disturbing implication of hereditary pig slaughter
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny twist on quaint local traditions. The hereditary aspect makes it absurdly morbid.

jokes.text: And he kisses you with that mouth.
jokes.setup: Watching Bob kiss meat and then his wife
jokes.punchline: Homer's disgusted observation about hygiene
jokes.explanation: Standard gross-out humor that works in context. Homer's practical disgust is relatable.

jokes.text: Chief, I tried to warn you. Sideshow bob hasn't lived here for months.
jokes.setup: Chief Wiggum apparently attacking the wrong house
jokes.punchline: Lou's matter-of-fact correction about their police incompetence
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum police incompetence. Lou's deadpan delivery of their failure works well.

jokes.text: Mark Wiggum, fat kid, played a lot of tetris.
jokes.setup: Wiggum seeing a Penn State banner
jokes.punchline: His random personal connection to the university through his cousin
jokes.explanation: Typical Wiggum tangent. The specific details make it funnier than generic randomness.

jokes.text: my heartfelt wish that they never return.
jokes.setup: Bob giving a toast honoring the Simpsons
jokes.punchline: His honest desire for them to leave forever
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bob moment - maintaining social niceties while expressing genuine malice.

jokes.text: Marge, don't be so olive garden. // Yeah, this is Italy. Look, the town drunk is two years old.
jokes.setup: Marge objecting to giving wine to Lisa
jokes.punchline: Homer's dismissal using restaurant chain as insult, Bart's supporting evidence
jokes.explanation: Using 'Olive Garden' as an adjective for fake Italian culture is brilliant. The toddler drunk escalates perfectly.

jokes.text: Look, the town drunk is two years old.
jokes.setup: Arguing about Italian drinking culture
jokes.punchline: Points out extremely young alcoholic as cultural norm
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about cultural differences in alcohol consumption. The specific age makes it more absurd.

jokes.text: More like jailed at sing sing.
jokes.setup: Previous line about Bob being 'hailed at this wingding'
jokes.punchline: Lisa's rhyming correction about Bob's criminal status
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay that maintains the rhyme scheme while making a serious point about Bob's criminality.

jokes.text: Drunken children tell the ugliest lies.
jokes.setup: Lisa exposing Bob's criminal past
jokes.punchline: Bob dismissing the truth as drunken childish lies
jokes.explanation: Bob's attempt to gaslight the situation. The phrase 'ugliest lies' when it's actually truth is perfect irony.

jokes.text: But darling, the boy drove me to murder by exonerating a harlequin who I framed for robbery at the Kwik-E-Mart.
jokes.setup: Bob trying to explain his attempted murder to his wife
jokes.punchline: His convoluted justification makes him sound even more guilty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bob verbosity making a terrible situation worse. His eloquent explanation only highlights his guilt.

jokes.text: Boy, that Sideshow Bob goes from zero to murder awfully fast.
jokes.setup: Bob immediately reverting to murderous intentions
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual observation about Bob's escalation pattern
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary on Bob's character pattern. Bart's nonchalant tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: At least this time it was Lisa who ruined Bob's life.
jokes.setup: Lisa exposed Bob's criminal past
jokes.punchline: Bart's relief that he's not the cause this time
jokes.explanation: Good sibling dynamics with Bart happy to pass the blame to Lisa for once.

jokes.text: It's called a hangover, sweetie. And it's an unavoidable part of life.
jokes.setup: Lisa feeling sick after drinking wine
jokes.punchline: Marge matter-of-factly explaining hangovers to her 8-year-old
jokes.explanation: Marge treating adult problems as normal childhood lessons. The casual parenting approach is funny.

jokes.text: What am i supposed to use it for... transporting water to distant urban centers?! Lazy romans.
jokes.setup: Lisa mentions they're driving on a Roman aqueduct
jokes.punchline: Homer dismissing the aqueduct's actual historical purpose as laziness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant historical ignorance from Homer. He knows what aqueducts do but thinks it's stupid, which is perfectly Homer.

jokes.text: Why do I keep trying to kill them?
jokes.setup: Bob in the midst of another murder attempt
jokes.punchline: His moment of existential questioning about his life choices
jokes.explanation: Perfect meta-moment where Bob questions his own recurring villain status. Brief but brilliant self-awareness.

jokes.text: Revenge is a dish best served family-style.
jokes.setup: The family joining Bob's vendetta
jokes.punchline: Twist on the famous revenge proverb with restaurant terminology
jokes.explanation: Clever twist on 'revenge is a dish best served cold.' The family-style reference works perfectly for the group vendetta.

jokes.text: I feel like I'm the Bourne Identity.
jokes.setup: Being chased through Rome with no car
jokes.punchline: Homer comparing himself to Jason Bourne
jokes.explanation: Straightforward pop culture reference. Works for Homer's character but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: They have that here, too?
jokes.setup: Learning Krusty is performing in an opera in Rome
jokes.punchline: Homer surprised that Italy invented opera
jokes.explanation: Good Homer ignorance about cultural origins. His American-centric worldview creates the humor.

jokes.text: Vendetta? What's that, an Italian vending machine?
jokes.setup: Simpsons explaining Bob's vendetta threat
jokes.punchline: Krusty's misunderstanding creates commercial pun
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay that fits Krusty's commercial mindset. The pun works but isn't exceptional.

jokes.text: I am the gayest super villain ever. Beware my scented candles.
jokes.setup: Homer doing Phantom of the Opera impression
jokes.punchline: His version emphasizes stereotypical gay characteristics
jokes.explanation: Homer's stereotypical take on the Phantom. The scented candles detail is funny but relies on gay stereotypes.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, it sounds like you're all taking a Napoli.
jokes.setup: Being corrected that Naples is called Napoli
jokes.punchline: Krusty's bad pun treating Napoli like 'nap-oli'
jokes.explanation: Typical Krusty bad pun. Perfect for his character as a hack comedian but not genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Well, I need someone to help me smuggle antiquities to America. Everyone, break off a piece and hide it on your person.
jokes.setup: Krusty appearing to rescue the Simpsons
jokes.punchline: His real motive is using them for art smuggling
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty moment - his 'heroic' rescue has selfish criminal motives. Good character consistency.
